title: Day 03 Quiz — Lists, Keys, and Reusable Components
description: Verify you understand map/filter/reduce, children, list rendering, keys, and component composition before Day 04.

questions.prompt: Why does React require a key prop when rendering a list of elements?

options.text: Keys encrypt list data for security
Keys help React identify which items changed, were added, or removed across re-renders
Keys are only needed for class components
Keys replace the need for useState in lists

questions.explanation: Keys give React a stable identity per list item so reconciliation can update the DOM efficiently and preserve component state correctly.

questions.prompt: Using the array index as key is always the best choice for dynamic lists where items can be reordered or deleted.
questions.explanation: Index keys break when list order changes — React may reuse the wrong DOM nodes and component state. Use stable unique IDs from data when possible.
map transforms each element. join concatenates with the separator.

questions.prompt: What does the children prop represent in React?

options.text: Only text nodes — JSX elements cannot be children
Content passed between opening and closing tags of a component
A built-in hook for nested routes
The parent component's state object

questions.explanation: children is whatever you nest inside a component — elements, text, fragments, or arrays. It enables composition patterns like Card wrapping arbitrary content.

questions.prompt: Which are good reasons to extract reusable form components? Select all that apply.

options.text: Consistent styling and accessibility across forms
Single place to fix bugs in input behavior
Eliminates the need for useState
Easier testing of isolated UI pieces

questions.explanation: Reusable components improve consistency and maintainability. State is still required for controlled inputs — extraction does not remove useState.

questions.prompt: To sum validation error counts in an array of fields, use {{blank1}} with an initial value of {{blank2}}.
questions.explanation: reduce accumulates a single value from an array — common for counts and aggregates.

questions.prompt: Complete the list render with a stable key from field.id.
questions.explanation: key must be on the outermost element returned from map — use a stable unique id from data, not Math.random().

questions.prompt: You have an array of error strings and only want non-empty messages. Which method fits best?

options.text: map()
filter()
reduce() only — filter cannot help
sort()

questions.explanation: filter keeps elements that pass a predicate — ideal for removing empty strings before rendering an error list.

questions.prompt: Why should reusable input components accept value and onChange as props instead of owning email/password state internally?
questions.sampleAnswer: The parent form owns state so the library stays reusable across different forms. value and onChange keep inputs controlled while letting each page manage its own state shape.
questions.explanation: Interviewers test whether you understand presentational vs container separation even before custom hooks on Day 06.

questions.prompt: Console warns: Each child in a list should have a unique key prop. You mapped errors but put key on an inner <span>. What is wrong?

options.text: Keys must be numbers only
key belongs on the outermost element returned from map, not a nested child
Lists cannot contain span elements
You must use useEffect to fix keys

questions.explanation: React reads key on the direct child of the array. Move key to the top-level element in the map callback return.
